figures.caption: Figure 4: SAE training convergence. Layer 8 (blue) encodes a denser feature set; deeper layers 12 (orange) and 16 (green) show sparser, more selective representations with more feature death.

Figure 3: Predicted vs. actual floor (log-log, 140 points). Left: Refined formula with g(α) (Pearson r = 0.93, MAPE = 93.9%). Right: Naive formula assuming one feature/dim (R2 = −0.04). Color = sparsity.
Figure 14: Training curves at different widths for six configurations. Dashed = predicted floors. Rapid convergence confirms geometric origin.
Figure 13: Normalized floor vs. dS/d ∗ S. All configurations collapse: floor drops sharply at dS = d∗S (dashed). This universal scaling confirms the phase transition.

Figure 5: (a) Feature importance follows a power law: the top ∼20 features dominate, with a cliff near rank ∼3,000 where thousands reach ∼10−7. This heavy tail is why compression works. (b) Predicted floor vs. width at layers 8, 12, 16. All layers agree (d∗S ∈ [1065, 1186]), converging near zero at dS = 1024.
Figure 8: Linear probe results. (a) Heatmap: all concepts survive compression. (b) ±3 pp shifts reflect reallocation. (c) All above chance (50%).

Figure 7: Two-component decomposition. (a) Linear fit (R2 = −1.982) fails. (b) Affine fit (R2 = 0.993): observed = 8.97× predicted + 0.623. Baseline B = 0.623: architectural floor; slope C = 8.97: amplification through transformer layers.
Figure 12: Zipf importance. Left: Linear scale. Right: Log-log confirms power law.
Figure 11: Accuracy heatmap (refined). Rows: α; cols: dT; panels: n. Green = > 99%.

Figure 1: Capacity function and critical width. Left: g(α) grows exponentially with sparsity; colored dots mark our toy model sparsity levels. Right: Critical width d∗S = F/g(α) shrinks as sparsity increases, since sparser features need fewer dimensions.

Figure 18: Distillation summary. Top left: eval curves with floor estimates. Top right: per-token KL floor vs. width. Bottom left: normalized observed vs. predicted floors. Bottom right: seed variance at dS = 128.
Figure 9: Effect of α on floor at dT = 5 for n ∈ {10, 20, 40}. Higher sparsity (more features/dim) yields lower floors. Solid = actual; dashed = predicted.
Figure 10: Prediction error by sparsity. Refined (colored): median > 90% accuracy; naive (gray): degrades at high α.
Table 3: Distillation loss floors. Raw KL summed across 512 positions ×T 2 = 4. Per-token KL = raw/2048. Normalized relative to dS = 128.
Figure 16: (a) Training loss for all widths. (b) Two seeds at dS = 128: floors differ by ∆ = 6.4 (0.24%), confirming the floor is deterministic.

Figure 17: Normalized predicted (SAE, dashed gray) vs. observed (distillation, solid red) floors. Both decrease monotonically; the widening gap reflects the constant baseline B dominating at larger widths (see Table 5).
Table 7: Reference values for g(α).
Figure 6: Distillation results. (a) Eval loss for all widths; narrower students plateau higher. (b) Floor decreases from 1.320 to 0.586 nats. Dotted line marks d∗S ≈ 1065.
Table 6: Linear probe accuracy (%) at layer 12. All concepts stay far above chance (50%).

Figure 2: Loss floor vs. student width across 48 configurations (rows: n; columns: α). Solid = actual (mean ± std, 20 seeds); dashed = formula (Eq. 2); dotted = d∗S. The formula captures both magnitude and shape.
Figure 15: Per-layer SAE curves: layers 8 (top), 12 (mid), 16 (bottom). Layer 8 has lower recon loss, higher L0, near-zero feature death.
Table 8: Student architectures. All share teacher’s depth (24), vocabulary (50,304), and positional encoding.
